posts.name: Clint S Q U I E R
posts.cooked:
To piggyback on the “super seconds” thread - which is great, I’ll pose the question of what are your favorite second wines of Bordeaux?

I don’t have much experience buying/drinking the second wines of Bordeaux - my thought is that for the price of a “famous” second wine such as Forts de Latour you can buy a 1st wine of a very, very good Chateau. It’s not often I see second wines for sale locally either and I’m not apt to buy them online. However, I have to believe there are some really good ones out there, especially from good vintages such as '05, '09 and '10.

Is this a category where a person can find some really good everyday drinkers? Is there a list of the second wines from the classified growths?
posts.display_username: Clint S Q U I E R

posts.name: Henry Kiichli
posts.cooked:
They tend to be over-valued/over-priced from the big chateaux.

I got some Demoiselle de Sociando Mallet and really liked it. Nice price, too.

posts.name: Michae1 P0wers
posts.cooked: I have been consistently disappointed, even when the prices for Carruades and Pavilion Rouge where acceptable. I do remember a good bottle of 2000 Pagodes de Cos many years ago, but it was under $30 so a decent deal.
Le Forts de Latour has always been an excellent wine, but my appetite for $150+ bottles of Bordeaux has waned.

posts.name: Nick Gangas
posts.cooked: No way would I ever spend the money for a top second label. I much rather get a quality chateau like Lynch, ducru or any number of wines.

posts.name: A. So
posts.cooked:
Nick Gangas:

No way would I ever spend the money for a top second label. I much rather get a quality chateau like Lynch, ducru or any number of wines.

+1.

posts.name: Ramon Cabrera
posts.cooked:
It’s not really a big interest for me, i.e. the 2nd wines of Bordeaux. Only because of their large drop down in quality from the first and, yes, the obscene pricing of the ones that I know of and I see out there available in the market.

But of the ones that I’ve tasted, I would be inclined to go with Clos du Marquis and La Dame du Montrose, as they seem to provide the more consistent good drinkers for their relative and respective values. Of course, others would argue that Clos du Maquis is really another wine of the property and not the second wine.

posts.name: Clint S Q U I E R
posts.cooked:
Forgetting the top labels for a minute, any from the 2nd-5th growths that fit in the $25-$60 range?

I remember buying a case of '01 Forts from Binnys for less than $40 a bottle and drank it like water. Whoops.
posts.display_username: Clint S Q U I E R

posts.name: Marc Frontario
posts.cooked: Lacoste borie is my huckleberry

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked:
The best I ever had was 1989 Bahans Haut Brion. It was about $20+ on release (HB about $100 and super-seconds about $40). That was a true value. It really was as good as the 2nds. I recently had a 1982 which was quite good too.

Overall, though, I think the value of these wines is questionable.

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
Its a pretty narrow set of circumstances where they are worth buying

a) great vintage
b) second wine had good vats allocated
c) fair pricing

It seems to me, that in recent years, the better estates want to extract a far larger premium for these.

The concept was that a Leoville Las Cases patron was supposed to be able to enjoy Clos du Marquis on a more regular, weeknight basis while young, while waiting for the grand vin to mature, which was saved for special occasions. So I used to personally buy and drink these, many years ago, when this was the value proposition, and the above conditions were true. Now, and not for a 10-12 years or so, has that been true – at least from my perspective.

I heard an anecdote from a broker that the 2nd labels are popular in Asia, for serving to guests, when the label is very important, and a sign of respect.

Outside of Bordeaux there are a few 2nd labels which have been excellent at times – Dominus’ Napanook and Pingus’s Flor de Pingus spring to mind.

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked:
Arv R:

It seems to me, that in recent years, the better estates want to extract a far larger premium for these.

Is this really true or is it just sticker shock on all Bordeaux? It looks to me like the ratio between Grand Vin and 2nd has gone up if anything. As I mentioned in the early 90’s the 1st growths’ 2nd wines (except Latour which had a different model) were ~20-25% of the Grand Vin price. At least in 2009/2010 it seems that this number was lower, say 15-20%. I’m not sure if this reflects pricing from the source or markups in the channel.

posts.name: Glen Gold
posts.cooked: I highly recommend Mondot, 2005 and 2009. Also La Dame de Montrose, 2010.

posts.name: Ed Steinway
posts.cooked:
We recently has a 2009 La Parde de Haut Bailly that was good. Around $35 or so.

Thanks,
Ed

posts.name: Ashish
posts.cooked:
I was not impressed by the 1998 Carruades de Lafite that I’ve tried. The 95 Les Forts de Latour was much better, but still not worth the current price (but definitely worth the <$50 release price). The only other one I have left is a 2000 Pavillon du Chateau Margaux that I have never tried. I’ve heard good things though.

In general, I prefer to go for the first wine of a slightly lower chateau. This is even more pronounced in Sauternes, where second wines from places like Rieussec can still be just as expensive as a Doisy Daene or Doisy Vedrines from an excellent vintage.

posts.name: b0b_c_stner
posts.cooked: Not so much because they resemble the first wine but because they tend to be solid wines that I enjoy and don’t break the bank: Sarget de Gruaud-Larose and Moulin Riche.

posts.name: Nick Gangas
posts.cooked:
Clint S Q U I E R:

Forgetting the top labels for a minute, any from the 2nd-5th growths that fit in the $25-$60 range?

I remember buying a case of '01 Forts from Binnys for less than $40 a bottle and drank it like water. Whoops.

When those deals pop up absolutely. My point is seconds are normally bad value.

posts.name: Luis Manuel Debuque Monreal Ermitaño
posts.cooked: Like some have already said, Les Forts, but, at current prices, I’d rather buy grand vins of other châteaux.

posts.name: Leonard Maran
posts.cooked: I agree with the prevailing opinion of “second wines” though back when I bought the 2000 vintage I did enjoy the second wines of Lafite Rothschild and Margaux. But of course that’s all over; they are not values anymore, in any sense. By the way Clos Marquis is not really a second wine of Leoville, as it is made from a different vineyard. I still have not had the 2000 Clos Marquis!

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
Craig G:
Arv R:

It seems to me, that in recent years, the better estates want to extract a far larger premium for these.

Is this really true or is it just sticker shock on all Bordeaux? It looks to me like the ratio between Grand Vin and 2nd has gone up if anything. As I mentioned in the early 90’s the 1st growths’ 2nd wines (except Latour which had a different model) were ~20-25% of the Grand Vin price. At least in 2009/2010 it seems that this number was lower, say 15-20%. I’m not sure if this reflects pricing from the source or markups in the channel.

You know, that’s a very good point, and one I didn’t really think through. If the estates are typically charging 3x-5x for the grand vin over the left over vats, then maybe I was being too harsh. And its really a function of the elite wines being bid up more than the 5th growths who might have been the markets/consumers competitor for a grand growths 2nd.

So after all my blather and pontification, I picked up a 2010 Pagodes today. Other vintages of Pagodes have been underwhelming, especially if price was considered, but I guess I’ll stash it away, hoping that it a peak year 2nd just might be better than an off vintage Cos which cost more anyways. We’ll see – I’m not overly optimistic.

posts.name: Jeff Vaughan
posts.cooked: I just picked up a bottle of 2010 l’esprit de Chevalier for $27.50. I am curious to see how it compares to Lanessan, Cambon la Pelouse, Senejac, etc. in this price range. Has anyone tried this wine or have thoughts on this bottling?

posts.name: John B a s k a m
posts.cooked: I used to enjoy Carruades de Lafite. After the whole Lafite / China craze took off, and once it became more expensive than Montrose, I sold all the bottles I owned. I have enjoyed the few Alter Ego(s) de Palmer I have had.

posts.name: Jonathan H
posts.cooked: My favourite would have to be Les Forts as well, but it is just not worth the price anymore. I also have a fondness for Pavillon Rouge. The 2004 is drinking incredibly well right now - and for the ~$40 I paid, a stunning value.

posts.name: Ted Alexander
posts.cooked:
Arv R:

Its a pretty narrow set of circumstances where they are worth buying

a) great vintage
b) second wine had good vats allocated
c) fair pricing

It seems to me, that in recent years, the better estates want to extract a far larger premium for these.

The concept was that a Leoville Las Cases patron was supposed to be able to enjoy Clos du Marquis on a more regular, weeknight basis while young, while waiting for the grand vin to mature, which was saved for special occasions. So I used to personally buy and drink these, many years ago, when this was the value proposition, and the above conditions were true. Now, and not for a 10-12 years or so, has that been true – at least from my perspective.

I heard an anecdote from a broker that the 2nd labels are popular in Asia, for serving to guests, when the label is very important, and a sign of respect.

Outside of Bordeaux there are a few 2nd labels which have been excellent at times – Dominus’ Napanook and Pingus’s Flor de Pingus spring to mind.

Do Rioja Reservas count as second wines? If so, how about Finca Ygay and Heredia Bosconia/ Tondonia?

posts.name: Kris_P@tten
posts.cooked: I have liked Dame Montrose, Rose de Gruaud, and Croix Beaucaillou…many vintages have been very reasonably priced.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
I have never chased and have rarely bought second labels. The association with the prime label means nothing to me if the wine does not stand on its own. Most are not that great, and many are not great values either.

A Chateau that produces a second label that has worked for me is Vieux Chateau Certain. As much as I love VCC, I will not plunk down the $350+ for the stellar 2010. The second label, La Gravette de Certan, produced an outstanding 2010 at all of $50. It’s the classic old school Pomerol that you expect from VCC, and backward to boot, but layered with so much stuffing. It easily competes with other Bordeaux in that $50 class, but on its own, is a much better wine and value than many first label Pomerols from 2010.

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
Ted Alexander:
Arv R:

Its a pretty narrow set of circumstances where they are worth buying

a) great vintage
b) second wine had good vats allocated
c) fair pricing

It seems to me, that in recent years, the better estates want to extract a far larger premium for these.

The concept was that a Leoville Las Cases patron was supposed to be able to enjoy Clos du Marquis on a more regular, weeknight basis while young, while waiting for the grand vin to mature, which was saved for special occasions. So I used to personally buy and drink these, many years ago, when this was the value proposition, and the above conditions were true. Now, and not for a 10-12 years or so, has that been true – at least from my perspective.

I heard an anecdote from a broker that the 2nd labels are popular in Asia, for serving to guests, when the label is very important, and a sign of respect.

Outside of Bordeaux there are a few 2nd labels which have been excellent at times – Dominus’ Napanook and Pingus’s Flor de Pingus spring to mind.

Do Rioja Reservas count as second wines? If so, how about Finca Ygay and Heredia Bosconia/ Tondonia?

I don’t think of the various bottlings in Rioja as 2nds wines but perhaps others might. Generally I thought of joven/crianza/reserva etc. as the regions way of showing how much aging a wine had. And typically, the better vats were the ones that got more time in the cellar before release. It does seem like some producers are moving away from this form of labelling though.

Robert: Thanks for the comment of the Gravette de Certan. I’ll keep it on the radar screen.

posts.name: Ramon Cabrera
posts.cooked:
Arv R:

I don’t think of the various bottlings in Rioja as 2nds wines but perhaps others might. Generally I thought of joven/crianza/reserva etc. as the regions way of showing how much aging a wine had. And typically, the better vats were the ones that got more time in the cellar before release. It does seem like some producers are moving away from this form of labelling though.

The counterpoint, Arv, to Rioja’s reserva/crianza as not representative of 2nd wines would be that they, or at least a few bodegas that I am aware of, would only make Gran Reserva wines in “exceptional” vintages. To me, that process, including the deliberate differentials in aging lengths, relegates the reservas/crianzas to 2nd/3rd wines.

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
Hmm. Does that mean the estates in France which offer a Tete du Cuvee are saying their regular wine is really a 2nd?

========

While one mulls that over…I think a better deal for consumers is to look not for the 2nds but other estates where the quality/production standards/personnel of the wine is similar but doesn’t come with the same label affiliation.

Examples of that might include Hacienda Monasterio instead of Flor de Pingus in Spain, or Moulin St Georges instead of Chapelle d’Ausone.

posts.name: Marc Frontario
posts.cooked:
J B a s k a m:

I used to enjoy Carruades de Lafite. After the whole Lafite / China craze took off, and once it became more expensive than Montrose, I sold all the bottles I owned. I have enjoyed the few Alter Ego(s) de Palmer I have had.

FYI alter ego is not a true second wine… just like clos de marquis is not for Las Cases…the true second wine for Las Cases is ‘Le Petit Lion’ du Marquis de Las Cases

posts.name: Alex Rychlewski
posts.cooked:
Marc,

I have great difficulty in imagining Clos du Marquis, les Forts de Latour, Alter Ego, etc. as anything other than second wines.

I think it’s simply a question of semantics and spin.

OK, so they come from a specific part of the estate. Got that. But they are a lesser wine made by one château. That makes them a second wine in my book, period. Of course, they can say whatever they want, if it helps them to charge more, or to make the second wines seem more glamorous.

Best regards,
Alex R.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked:
Spin or not, I consider Clos du Marquis a second wine, and a delicious one at that. The only factor that makes me think of it as a separate entity is that is bears little or no resemblance to Las Cases in terms of style.

Dame de Montrose is one of my favorite Bordeaux to drink young-ish. When I can find it at a good price I buy.

posts.name: Mark Mason
posts.cooked:
I thought I’ve seen the Carruades vineyard on Bordeaux maps. Do they blend in grand cru grapes with these to make Carruades a true second?

When I first got into wine, the 1983 Carruades were my special occasion wines, I’ll bet I bought three cases of it! I also loaded up on the 2000, but have sold most of it.

posts.name: Matt Fleming
posts.cooked:
Leonard Maran:

By the way Clos Marquis is not really a second wine of Leoville, as it is made from a different vineyard. I still have not had the 2000 Clos Marquis!

The same is true for many second wines from BDX. New plots or plots known to under perform or have a style all their own, get the second label. Les Forts is one.

It was a shame that greed dumped La Tour Haut Brion in with La Mission a few years back. That was always a remarkable wine at a reasonable price. I treasure my few remaining bottles.

posts.name: Thomas Keim
posts.cooked:
Of course there are terrific second labels - You just have to be more selective with them as the quality can be more up and down than the main wine.

and - In today’s pricing market, I am seeing way too many second labels priced like their flagships from various wholesalers and retailers across the country - like it’s the same wine.

It’s always best to taste before you buy a second label - unless the press is going nuts across the board and it’s cheap on the futures market -

posts.name: Jeff Vaughan
posts.cooked:
Jeff Vaughan:

I just picked up a bottle of 2010 l’esprit de Chevalier for $27.50. I am curious to see how it compares to Lanessan, Cambon la Pelouse, Senejac, etc. in this price range. Has anyone tried this wine or have thoughts on this bottling?

While this seemed slightly on the modern side of the spectrum, it was pretty tasty. I picked up a 12 pack of .375’s for 14 each.

2010 L’Esprit de Chevalier - France, Bordeaux, Graves, Pessac-Léognan (11/20/2015)
Dark in color. Black fruits, coffee, and char initially. With air, a bit of pencil shavings and bell pepper emerges. On the palate there is a fair amount of dark fruit but the wine is not heavy at all. Good acid and medium tannin. As you’d expect, the wine is not as dense or structured as the primary bottling but this is a nice wine. I am going to pick up some more. (89 pts.)

posts.name: Marc Frontario
posts.cooked:
Craig G:

The best I ever had was 1989 Bahans Haut Brion. It was about $20+ on release (HB about $100 and super-seconds about $40). That was a true value. It really was as good as the 2nds. I recently had a 1982 which was quite good too.

Overall, though, I think the value of these wines is questionable.

We cracked an '01 Bahans haut brion at a local wine bar 4 years back for about $45…a top ten Bordeaux for me…I have tasted the big brother 3X’s …I would rather have the bahans

posts.name: Sanjay Nandurkar

posts.name: Marc Frontario
posts.cooked: Alfert and I popped an '04 reserve de comtesse 4 years back…it was stellar…very pauillac

posts.name: Nigel Groundwater
posts.cooked:
Marc Frontario:
Craig G:

The best I ever had was 1989 Bahans Haut Brion. It was about $20+ on release (HB about $100 and super-seconds about $40). That was a true value. It really was as good as the 2nds. I recently had a 1982 which was quite good too.

Overall, though, I think the value of these wines is questionable.

We cracked an '01 Bahans haut brion at a local wine bar 4 years back for about $45…a top ten Bordeaux for me…I have tasted the big brother 3X’s …I would rather have the bahans

The alter ego is great also…I love '02 and '04

Just had the 2010 second wine of sociando…$20…nice

Clos du Marquis is another mistaken second wine…but ‘le petit lion’ is the second wine of Las Cases

Le Petit Lion is indeed the ‘designated’ 2nd wine of Las Cases BUT it’s new having been around for less than 10 years whereas Clos du Marquis is a centurion at least IIRC. Of course Bahans de Haut-Brion is now called Le Clarence de Haut-Brion and still designated the second wine.

Prior to Le Petit Lion, Clos du Marquis was usually considered the 2nd wine of the estate even though it was [substantially] from its own separate ‘Clos’ although I recall several times over the years, before Le Petit Lion was created, when parts of the normal cepage of the Grand Vin e.g. the Cabernet Franc were ‘demoted’ to Clos du Marquis - just as today such decisions are presumably what also makes up Le Petit Lion along with parts of the vineyard that would not be used for the Grand Vin for reasons e.g. of vine age.

However one wants to designate it [similar 'arguments could be applied to other famous seconds] IMO Clos du Marquis was always one of the superior wines amongst the top ‘seconds’. Currently happily drinking the 96.

As for the rest the most reliably good and most expensive was, as others have mentioned, Les Forts de Latour although up to 2000 most of the second wines of the premier and deuxieme GCs including Les Forts were reasonable value. Of course since then we have seen insane values for Les Carruades [along with other wines in the Lafite Rothschild Group] while other wines have simply followed the Bordeaux spiral.

The only second I have bought since 2000 was the 2005 Dame de Montrose which, en primeur, was still good value and a very decent wine.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
I’ve never found that to be a category worth pursuing - oddly high prices relative to the quality, yet I don’t really perceive much prestige factor to them either.

Clos du Marquis is good - I agree that it’s not really a “second wine” in the normal sense of the term, but I guess people have different opinions about that.

posts.name: Robert Alfert

I do not buy a second label for the sake of the brand. The wine needs to stand on its own.

I like what La Gravette de Certan is doing, the 2009 and 2010 are strong! And decently priced at about $50 or so.

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
PXL_20231014_0102301061920×1661 219 KB

I’ve been very enthused about the 2016 Le Marquis de Calon Segur [St Estephe] 14% abv. Better on day 2, but still darn good popped and poured. Vigorous, masculine (!) flavor depth, elements of oak, and hefty structure especially when cool. Merlot driven, but still a serious wine, and one I would expect to hold well for a decade after the vintage. Just as good as the 2000 La Dame de Montrose was. A- in my ledger.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Another vote for Dame de Montrose for me. I’d add Croix Canon in recent vintages, too.

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
10000073691920×1855 337 KB

Here’s a curiousity I had never seen/tasted before - the 2019 Folie de Chauvin [St Emilion] which is the second wine of Chauvin. I had purchased various bottlings from Chauvin in the 1998-2009 era, when it was more moderniste/garagiste leaning but had not any recent vintages. The estate had some ownership linkage with Cheval Blanc once upon a time, so the old labels, during the prior Ondet ownership era were very similar to Cheval Blanc. But after the Ondet sisters ‘sold up’ a decade or so ago, the new owners - the Cazes family of great Medoc fame - reverted back to a much older styled label so everything looks different packaging wise. In the winery, they downgraded some vats/plots to a second wine while also aiming for more freshness. The result here shows 13.5% abv on the label, which for 2019, suggests the changes had impact. I found this ruby red merlot to be a sound, generally balanced example. Oak is minimal/resolved, acidity is aligned with red strawberry fruit, and the tannin only starts to pull ahead on day 3 when the fruit starts fading. No sediment. Agglomerated cork, perhaps of some technical kind, but not DIAM. I enjoyed this and would assign this ready to drink right bank a B on my card; I suspect I will not see this again though. This bottle came over via RTM Imports, a Texan agent for some big chains (HEB, Albertsons, etc) – I think they are mostly a paperwork / service provider like MHW. And between tarriffs and the wine slump, I don’t think wine retailers want to allocate capital to niches like this.

I was a little surprised that Stephen Brook’s 3rd edition Bordeaux book didn’t cover the recent ownership chain here. Many years ago on the BWE board we had quite the discussion of the luxe / tete de cuvee here, which was one of the last flourishes of prior owners.

posts.name: Nick Templeton
posts.cooked: Don’t think that it has been mentioned but Ségla, the second wine of Rauzan Ségla, is very good and moderately priced, at least in Europe. Agree too regarding La Gravette de Certan, the second wine of Vieux Château Certan, another relative bargain.
